objections.objection: Costs are listed qualitatively but lack any per-unit or aggregate quantification and do not distinguish transition (years 1–4) from steady-state fiscal exposures. For example, the item ‘teacher retention incentive costs’ cites the Magyar Falu Program (5 billion HUF for 100 settlements) but the scenario does not translate that anchor into per-settlement or per-position budgets or show how incentive costs scale with differing vacancy rates; likewise ‘capital-maintenance/backlog costs’ cite a Széchenyi 2020 total (24 billion HUF across 68 institutions) but never annualize or allocate backlog cost per small-school building. This omission prevents any fiscal-sensitivity testing (e.g., how costs change if cohorts decline 2% vs 8%/year) and leaves the claim that avoided consolidation ‘offsets’ some costs untestable.
objections.suggested_revision: Replace the qualitative list with a simple bottom-up fiscal model: (a) define per-unit parameters (teacher-training cost per teacher trained; annual retention incentive per vacancy filled; capital-maintenance backlog per school; per-km and per-student transport subsidy); (b) annualize one-off items over realistic amortization periods to produce year-by-year cash flows for transition (years 1–4) and steady-state (year 5+); (c) produce sample numeric scenarios (low/medium/high) driven by cohort-decline rates (e.g., −1%/yr, −3%/yr, −6%/yr) and compute net fiscal impact and NPV at 3 discount rates. Use the Magyar Falu and Széchenyi anchors to calibrate parameter ranges and show sensitivity of total budget lines to vacancy and cohort assumptions.

objections.objection: Key cost drivers for centralizing upper grades are under-specified and unbounded: the scenario names ‘one-off capital investment for designating/expanding ISCED2 capacity’ and cites a single-route transport precedent (~35 million HUF/year for ~50 students) but does not estimate per-seat capital costs, the number of receiving sites needed under alternative consolidation footprints, or how transport and capital costs interact (e.g., longer routes require more buses and earlier capital spending). There is no sensitivity to scale (transferring 200 vs 2,000 pupils has very different unit and marginal costs), nor to phasing (construction lead times and interim transport costs), so fiscal exposure and breakeven vs local retention cannot be assessed.
objections.suggested_revision: Add a scalable costing module: (a) estimate per-seat capital-expansion cost ranges for ISCED2 receiving sites (low/medium/high based on refurbishment vs new-build) and show totals for plausible consolidation scenarios (e.g., consolidate 10, 50, 200 village schools); (b) compute transport costs by route: per-bus annual cost, riders per bus, average km, with sensitivity to fuel/driver-wage inflation; (c) model transition-year costs separately (temporary transport, interim classroom leasing, accelerated construction premium) and steady-state operating costs; (d) present breakeven comparisons (cost of maintaining village schools under multigrade + per-pupil subsidy vs cost of consolidation) under at least three demographic trajectories.

objections.objection: The scenario recognizes long-run community fiscal effects (property-value decline, tax-base shrinkage) but treats them as ‘largely unmonetized’. Omitting quantified municipal revenue and service-cost feedbacks is a major flaw: closures can create persistent municipal fiscal shortfalls that change the net public-cost calculus (e.g., higher per-capita service unit costs, reduced municipal capacity to fund transport or repurposing). Without modelling these dynamic feedbacks the scenario's claim about net savings from consolidation is unanchored.
objections.suggested_revision: Incorporate a simple dynamic municipal-fiscal feedback model: (a) estimate plausible ranges for property-value declines (e.g., −5%, −10%, −20%) and translate into municipal tax-revenue loss over 5–10 years under Hungarian local-tax rules; (b) estimate increased unit costs for remaining local services (per-capita rises) and any higher-level fiscal transfers adjustments; (c) present sensitivity runs showing net public-sector fiscal outcome (central + municipal) of closure vs retention after 5 and 10 years. Use these outputs to decide whether TEIP-style mitigations are fiscally sufficient or require additional compensating transfers.

objections.objection: The registry and standards proposal omits a concrete, itemized cost estimate and fails to allocate fiscal responsibility across tiers. It lists ‘one-off registry-design’ and ‘recurring registry-operation’ costs but does not specify staffing FTEs, IT hosting and maintenance, data-acquisition costs from KSH/Klebelsberg, legal-compliance overhead, or the independent-audit budget — nor does it show how costs will be apportioned (ministry, tankerület, KSH), creating high risk of underfunding and discontinuity.
objections.suggested_revision: Produce a bottom-up budget for the registry and standardization package: (a) list required staff FTEs (project manager, data engineers, legal/GDPR officer, helpdesk, auditor) and market salary ranges; (b) estimate IT capital and annual hosting/licensing costs and data-collection fieldwork costs; (c) annualize one-off legal/design costs and list independent-audit costs; (d) propose explicit funding allocation (named budget line in Ministry of Education and/or KSH, plus contingency) and a 5-year cash-flow table showing set-up vs operating-year budgets; (e) include contingency scenarios if political funding drops (e.g., 25% cut) showing minimum-operational vs full-operational cost levels.
